content.text: The status page is the public face of your incident response. Customers, partners, and dependent systems consult it throughout an incident to decide whether to wait, escalate, or activate contingency plans. Getting the update cadence wrong — posting too rarely, too vaguely, or too late — converts a technical incident into a trust incident. This topic defines the update schedule, the content structure for each phase, and how to automate delivery so the cadence holds even under the worst pressure.

attrs.alt: Status page update timeline diagram showing SEV1 updates every 30 minutes and SEV2 every 60 minutes, plus required content for each phase: investigating, identified, monitoring, resolved

content.text: Why Cadence Matters More Than Content
Customers do not need to know your internal hypothesis. They need to know two things:
when they will hear from you next
. The predictable rhythm of updates is what prevents:
Support ticket floods — customers open tickets when they cannot tell if you know about the problem
Social media escalation — silence reads as indifference; a brief update stops the narrative from forming without you
Executive escalation spirals — a VP who saw a status page update 10 minutes ago does not page the CTO
Loss of contractual SLA — many enterprise contracts require notification within a defined window
update — even 'We are still investigating and have no new findings; next update in 30 minutes' — is always better than silence.
T+120 or end of business day

is a hard constraint — not a target. If the first SEV1 update is not posted by T+30, your Comms Liaison bot should post a holding message automatically. A holding message is better than missing the window:

Investigating — Inference API Latency

We are aware of reports of elevated latency and errors affecting the Inference API. Our engineering team is actively investigating. We will provide an update within 30 minutes.
Use this phase when you know something is wrong but have not confirmed the root cause. Required fields:
What is affected (service name, not internal component names)
What users experience (errors, slow responses, no access)
What your team is doing (actively investigating)
When the next update will arrive (exact time, UTC)

Investigating — {Service Name}

We are investigating reports of {user-facing symptom} affecting {service name}. Some {users / API requests / workflows} may experience {errors / elevated latency / partial unavailability}. Our engineering team is actively working to identify the root cause. We will provide an update by {HH:MM UTC}.
Use this phase when you know the root cause but have not yet deployed a fix. Customers appreciate the honesty — it signals you have a path forward:

Identified — {Service Name}

We have identified the cause of the {symptom} affecting {service name}. {One sentence on the nature of the issue — no internal system names, no blame.} Our team is working on a fix and expects to begin the deployment shortly. We will provide an update by {HH:MM UTC}.
unless you are certain — a missed ETA is worse than no ETA.
Use this phase after deploying a fix, while you watch metrics recover. It tells customers the worst is over:

Monitoring — {Service Name}

We have deployed a fix for the {symptom} affecting {service name}. The service is recovering and we are monitoring to confirm full restoration. Some users may continue to experience intermittent issues during this period. We will provide a final update once the service is fully restored.
Post the resolution update as soon as you are confident the incident is over. Include the full picture customers need for their own post-incident reports:

Resolved — {Service Name}

{Service name} has been fully restored as of {HH:MM UTC}. The incident lasted approximately {N} hours and affected {description of scope}. {One sentence on what caused it, written in plain language.} We apologise for the disruption this has caused. A post-incident report will be published within five business days.
Relying on humans to remember a 30-minute clock during an active SEV1 at 3 AM is a design flaw. Automate the reminders and, where possible, the posting itself.
Automated Holding Message on Missed Window

HOLDING_TEMPLATE = (
    "We continue to investigate the {symptom} affecting {service}. "
    "Our engineering team is actively working on resolution. "
    "We will provide an update by {next_update}."
)

async def _post_holding_message_if_overdue(
    cadence: StatusPageCadence,
    slack_client,
    now: datetime,
) -> None:
    overdue_by = (now - cadence.next_update_due_at(now)).seconds // 60
    if overdue_by < 5:
        return  # grace period — comms liaison may be typing

    # Auto-post holding message via Statuspage API
    next_update = (now + timedelta(minutes=cadence.interval_minutes)).strftime("%H:%M UTC")
    body = HOLDING_TEMPLATE.format(
        symptom=cadence.active_incident_symptom,
        service=cadence.service_name,
        next_update=next_update,
    )
    statuspage.post_update(
        incident_id=cadence.statuspage_incident_id,
        body=body,
        status=IncidentStatus.INVESTIGATING,
    )
    cadence.last_posted = now
    await slack_client.chat_postMessage(
        channel=cadence.channel_id,
        text=(
            f":warning: Auto-posted holding message to status page "
            f"(window was overdue by {overdue_by} min). "
            f"<@{cadence.comms_liaison_slack_id}> please follow up with a detailed update."
        ),
    )
What to Exclude from Status Page Updates
Status pages are public. Everything you post is permanently visible, can be screenshot, and will appear in post-mortem retrospectives and sometimes in the press. Apply this filter before posting:
If wrong, trust damage is compounded
Missed ETAs are worse than no ETA
Looks unprofessional; may be legally sensitive
"We are working with our providers to resolve this."
Meaningless to customers; may reveal SLA limits
Describe user-facing impact in plain language
May aid attackers; requires Legal review first
"We are investigating a security-related issue." — then stop until Legal approves more

Status page subscribers opt in to email or SMS notifications on every update. For a major incident with many updates, this can mean 6-8 emails to every subscriber over two hours. Manage this deliberately:
Do not enable subscriber notifications for every minor SEV3 update — only for significant status changes
Use the statuspage.io 'deliver_notifications' flag to control which updates trigger emails
For the resolution update, always send subscriber notifications — it closes the loop and reduces follow-up tickets
If you post a holding message (bot auto-post), set deliver_notifications=False to avoid spamming subscribers with no-new-info messages

def post_statuspage_update(
    client: StatusPageClient,
    incident_id: str,
    body: str,
    status: IncidentStatus,
    is_significant: bool = True,
) -> None:
    """
    Significant updates (identified, monitoring, resolved) notify subscribers.
    Holding messages and routine investigating updates do not.
    """
    significant_statuses = {
        IncidentStatus.IDENTIFIED,
        IncidentStatus.MONITORING,
        IncidentStatus.RESOLVED,
    }
    deliver = is_significant and status in significant_statuses
    client.post_update(
        incident_id=incident_id,
        body=body,
        status=status,
        deliver_notifications=deliver,
    )

StatusPage.io and similar tools allow you to mark individual service components as degraded, partially unavailable, or down. This is more informative than a single top-level status for multi-component GenAI systems:
Latency elevated due to downstream inference slowness
Only mark components that are genuinely affected. Marking everything as degraded 'to be safe' trains customers to ignore your status page.
Post-Incident Report on the Status Page

Within five business days of resolution, post a post-incident report (PIR) to the status page. Customers with SLAs, compliance requirements, or their own post-mortems need it. This is different from your internal post-mortem — it is written for an external technical audience:

## Post-Incident Report: Inference API Latency — 2024-03-15

### Summary
On 2024-03-15 from 14:18 to 14:57 UTC (39 minutes), the Inference API experienced elevated
latency and errors affecting approximately 40% of requests in the us-east-1a region.

### Timeline
- **14:18 UTC** — Automated monitoring detected P99 latency exceeding 5 seconds.
- **14:20 UTC** — Incident declared; engineering team began investigation.
- **14:35 UTC** — Root cause identified: memory limit misconfiguration on inference pods.
- **14:40 UTC** — Traffic rerouted away from affected zone while fix was prepared.
- **14:45 UTC** — Latency began recovering.
- **14:57 UTC** — Full service restored; incident resolved.

### Root Cause
A memory limit configuration in the inference pod deployment was set lower than the actual
memory requirement, causing pods to be OOM-killed under normal load. The configuration drift
was introduced during a routine infrastructure update.

### Impact
- Duration: 39 minutes
- Affected requests: approximately 40% in us-east-1a
- Affected customers: enterprise customers with primary workloads in us-east-1a

### Corrective Actions
1. Memory limits corrected and validated against actual usage profiles.
2. Automated memory limit drift detection added to CI pipeline.
3. Regional traffic rerouting automated for future OOM events.
SEV1 status page updates must be posted every 30 minutes starting at T+30; SEV2 every 60 minutes starting at T+60
A minimal holding message beats silence — customers need confirmation you know about the problem
Four status phases: Investigating (no root cause), Identified (root cause known), Monitoring (fix deployed), Resolved
Never include internal system names, unconfirmed root cause, specific resolution ETAs, or blame in external updates
Use component-level statuses to show which parts of the system are affected — do not mark everything degraded 'to be safe'
Automate the cadence with a reminder bot; auto-post holding messages when the comms liaison misses the window
Control subscriber notifications — only send emails for significant phase transitions, not every holding message
Publish a post-incident report within five business days for SEV1 and SEV2 incidents
